Creative Development.prompt: This topic covers collaboration in computing for AP Computer Science Principles students (CED: CRD-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.1). Students should understand that collaboration is essential in software development and leads to better programs. Key concepts: diverse perspectives improve program design and help identify bias; effective collaboration includes pair programming, code reviews, and team-based development; online tools (version control, shared documents, communication platforms) facilitate collaboration; computing innovations are often developed by teams with complementary skills (designers, developers, testers). Students should understand how to give and receive constructive feedback, divide tasks effectively, and recognize that programs benefit from multiple viewpoints. Connect to the Create Performance Task where collaboration is documented. Vocabulary: pair programming, code review, version control, iterative development, feedback, team roles, diverse perspectives.

Creative Development.skill: CRD-2.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.2) Creative Development - 1.2 Program Function and Purpose
Creative Development.prompt: This topic covers program function and purpose for AP CSP students (CED: CRD-2.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.2). Students should understand the distinction between a program's purpose (the problem it solves or creative expression it enables) and its function (what it actually does, its inputs, outputs, and behavior). Key concepts: a computing innovation can be physical (drone), non-physical (algorithm), or conceptual (social media); every program processes inputs to produce outputs; programs can have intended and unintended effects on users and society; identifying the purpose helps guide design decisions. Students should describe what a program does (function), why it exists (purpose), and analyze both beneficial and harmful effects. Connect to the Explore Performance Task: students must analyze a computing innovation's purpose, function, and impact. Vocabulary: purpose, function, input, output, computing innovation, user, program behavior, intended effect.

Creative Development.skill: CRD-2.C (Unit 1, Topic 1.3) Creative Development - 1.3 Program Design and Development
Creative Development.prompt: This topic covers program design and development for AP CSP students (CED: CRD-2.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.3). Students should understand the iterative development process used to create programs. Key concepts: development starts with understanding the problem and identifying requirements; design tools include pseudocode, flowcharts, and storyboards; incremental development builds the program in small, testable pieces; the development process is iterative (design, implement, test, refine, repeat); program requirements may change during development. Students should plan programs before coding, break problems into manageable sub-problems, and understand that good design reduces errors and improves maintainability. Documentation throughout development helps others understand design decisions. Connect to the Create Performance Task where students document their development process. Vocabulary: iterative development, incremental, pseudocode, flowchart, requirements, design, prototype, refinement, documentation.

Creative Development.skill: CRD-2.H (Unit 1, Topic 1.4) Creative Development - 1.4 Identifying and Correcting Errors
Creative Development.prompt: This topic covers identifying and correcting errors for AP CSP students (CED: CRD-2.H, Unit 1, Topic 1.4). Students should understand the types of programming errors and strategies for finding and fixing them. Key concepts: syntax errors violate language rules and prevent compilation/execution; logic errors produce incorrect results but the program runs; runtime errors cause the program to crash during execution (e.g., division by zero); overflow errors occur when a value exceeds the storage capacity. Debugging strategies: using print/display statements to trace values, testing with boundary and edge cases, rubber duck debugging (explaining code aloud), using a debugger tool, and reading error messages carefully. Students should categorize errors, apply systematic debugging approaches, and understand that testing cannot prove a program is error-free but can reveal errors. Vocabulary: syntax error, logic error, runtime error, overflow error, debugging, testing, edge case, boundary case, trace.

Data.prompt: This topic covers binary numbers for AP CSP students (CED: DAT-1.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.1). Students should understand how computers represent all data using binary (base-2) number systems. Key concepts: binary uses only 0 and 1 (bits); converting between binary and decimal; n bits can represent 2^n different values; a byte is 8 bits; numbers, text (ASCII/Unicode), images (pixels with RGB values), and sound are all represented in binary. Students should convert between binary, decimal, and hexadecimal; calculate the number of bits needed to represent a given number of values; understand that all digital data is ultimately stored as sequences of bits. Overflow occurs when a value needs more bits than available. Analog data must be digitized (sampled and quantized) for computer processing, which involves approximation. Vocabulary: binary, bit, byte, decimal, hexadecimal, ASCII, Unicode, RGB, digitization, overflow, base-2, sampling.

This topic covers data compression for AP CSP students (CED: DAT-1.D, Unit 2, Topic 2.2). Students should understand why and how data is compressed to reduce storage space and transmission time. Key concepts: lossless compression reduces file size without losing any information and is perfectly reversible (e.g., ZIP, PNG); lossy compression permanently removes some data to achieve greater compression ratios, and the original cannot be fully restored (e.g., JPEG, MP3); the trade-off between file size and quality. Students should understand run-length encoding as a simple lossless technique, recognize when lossy vs. lossless compression is appropriate (lossy for media, lossless for text and code), and calculate compression ratios. The choice of compression method depends on the use case: medical images need lossless, streaming music can use lossy. Vocabulary: compression, lossless, lossy, compression ratio, run-length encoding, redundancy, file size, quality, decompression.

Data.skill: DAT-2.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.3) Data - 2.3 Extracting Information from Data
Data.prompt: This topic covers extracting information from data for AP CSP students (CED: DAT-2.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.3). Students should understand how large datasets are processed to discover patterns, trends, and insights. Key concepts: data is collected, cleaned, and analyzed to extract meaningful information; metadata is data about data (e.g., photo timestamp, file size); large datasets enable discovery of patterns that small samples cannot reveal; correlation does not imply causation; data can be filtered, sorted, and visualized to reveal trends. Students should understand challenges with large datasets: incomplete data, invalid entries, bias in collection methods, and privacy concerns. Tools for analysis include spreadsheets, databases, and programming. Visualizations (charts, graphs, maps) communicate findings effectively but can be misleading if scales or representations are manipulated. Vocabulary: data, information, metadata, correlation, causation, filtering, sorting, trend, pattern, bias, visualization, cleaning.

Data.skill: DAT-2.D (Unit 2, Topic 2.4) Data - 2.4 Using Programs with Data
Data.prompt: This topic covers using programs with data for AP CSP students (CED: DAT-2.D, Unit 2, Topic 2.4). Students should understand how programs can be used to process, transform, and analyze datasets. Key concepts: programs can automate data processing tasks that would be impractical to do manually; programs can compute statistics (mean, median, mode, standard deviation), search and filter records, and generate visualizations; using programs to analyze data enables answering questions and making predictions. Students should understand that programs can process data from files, APIs, and databases; that data must often be cleaned and transformed before analysis; and that the results of data analysis depend on the quality and completeness of the source data. Iterative refinement of analysis often leads to better questions and deeper insights. Vocabulary: data processing, automation, statistics, dataset, query, transformation, analysis, API, database, prediction, refinement.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-1.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.1) Algorithms and Programming - 3.1 Variables and Assignments
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers variables and assignments for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-1.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.1). Students should understand that variables are named storage locations that hold values which can change during program execution. Key concepts: assignment uses the arrow operator in pseudocode (a <- 5) or equals sign in most languages; the right side is evaluated first, then stored in the left-side variable; variables can store numbers, strings, and booleans; variable names should be meaningful and descriptive. Students should trace through sequential assignment statements, predict variable values after a series of operations, and understand that assignment overwrites the previous value. Swapping two variables requires a temporary variable. Important: assignment is not the same as mathematical equality (a <- a + 1 is valid in programming). Vocabulary: variable, assignment, value, expression, initialization, overwrite, swap, temporary variable, identifier.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-1.B (Unit 3, Topic 3.2) Algorithms and Programming - 3.2 Data Abstraction
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers data abstraction for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-1.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.2). Students should understand that data abstraction manages complexity by grouping related data and hiding implementation details. Key concepts: lists (arrays) store ordered collections of elements accessed by index; data abstraction allows programs to work with complex data without knowing its internal representation; abstract data types define what operations can be performed without specifying how they are implemented. Students should create and use lists to store multiple related values, understand that abstraction reduces complexity and makes programs easier to develop and maintain, and recognize that choosing appropriate data representations affects program efficiency and clarity. Connect to the Create Performance Task where students must demonstrate data abstraction. Vocabulary: abstraction, list, array, index, data structure, implementation, interface, encapsulation, complexity management.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-2.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.3) Algorithms and Programming - 3.3 Mathematical Expressions
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers mathematical expressions for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.3). Students should understand how to use arithmetic operators in programs to compute values. Key concepts: standard operators include addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), division (/), and modulo (MOD); the order of operations follows standard mathematical precedence; integer division vs. floating-point division; the MOD operator returns the remainder of division and is useful for determining even/odd, cycling through values, and extracting digits. Students should evaluate expressions step by step, predict the result of complex arithmetic expressions, and use parentheses to control evaluation order. Common applications: converting units, computing averages, calculating percentages, and determining divisibility. Vocabulary: arithmetic operator, expression, operand, precedence, MOD, integer division, remainder, evaluation, order of operations.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-2.C (Unit 3, Topic 3.4) Algorithms and Programming - Strings
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers strings for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.4). Students should understand that strings are ordered sequences of characters used to represent text data. Key concepts: strings can be created using quotes; concatenation joins two strings together; the length of a string is the number of characters it contains; individual characters can be accessed by their position (index); substrings extract a portion of a string. Students should manipulate strings using concatenation, determine string length, extract characters and substrings, and understand that strings are a fundamental data type for processing text input, displaying output, and working with textual data. Common operations: searching for a character or substring, comparing strings, and building strings from parts. Vocabulary: string, character, concatenation, length, index, substring, text, sequence, string operations, comparison.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-2.E (Unit 3, Topic 3.5) Algorithms and Programming - 3.5 Boolean Expressions
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers boolean expressions for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.E, Unit 3, Topic 3.5). Students should understand that boolean expressions evaluate to either true or false and are used to make decisions in programs. Key concepts: relational operators (=, !=, <, >, <=, >=) compare values; logical operators (AND, OR, NOT) combine boolean expressions; truth tables show all possible outcomes for logical operators; AND is true only when both operands are true; OR is true when at least one operand is true; NOT reverses the value. Students should build and evaluate compound boolean expressions, construct truth tables, and apply De Morgan's Laws (NOT(a AND b) = NOT a OR NOT b). Boolean expressions are the foundation of conditionals and loop conditions. Vocabulary: boolean, true, false, relational operator, logical operator, AND, OR, NOT, truth table, De Morgan's Laws, compound expression.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-2.G (Unit 3, Topic 3.6) Algorithms and Programming - 3.6 Conditionals
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers conditionals for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.G, Unit 3, Topic 3.6). Students should understand that conditional statements allow programs to make decisions and execute different code paths based on boolean conditions. Key concepts: IF statements execute a block only when the condition is true; IF-ELSE provides two-way branching; the condition is a boolean expression; conditionals enable programs to respond differently to different inputs and situations. Students should trace through conditional logic, predict which code block executes for given values, and write conditionals to implement decision-making. Common applications: input validation, categorizing data, game logic, and user interface responses. Understanding conditionals is essential for the Create Performance Task. Vocabulary: conditional, IF, IF-ELSE, condition, boolean expression, branching, selection, control flow, decision, code path.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-2.I (Unit 3, Topic 3.7) Algorithms and Programming - 3.7 Nested Conditionals
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers nested conditionals for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.I, Unit 3, Topic 3.7). Students should understand that conditionals placed inside other conditionals create multi-level decision structures. Key concepts: nested conditionals check additional conditions within an already-selected branch; they enable more complex decision-making than simple IF-ELSE; the order of conditions matters; deeply nested conditionals can often be simplified using compound boolean expressions (AND, OR). Students should trace through nested IF-ELSE structures, determine which branch executes for various input values, and recognize when nested conditionals can be refactored into simpler equivalent forms. Common use cases: grading systems with multiple criteria, multi-step validation, and decision trees. Best practice: avoid excessive nesting (more than 3 levels) for readability. Vocabulary: nested conditional, inner condition, outer condition, decision tree, branching depth, refactoring, compound condition.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-2.J (Unit 3, Topic 3.8) Algorithms and Programming - 3.8 Iteration
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers iteration (loops) for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.J, Unit 3, Topic 3.8). Students should understand that iteration allows a program to repeat a block of code multiple times. Key concepts: REPEAT n TIMES loops execute the body exactly n times; REPEAT UNTIL (condition) loops execute until the condition becomes true; loop body is the code that repeats; iteration is essential for processing lists, searching for values, and performing repetitive computations. Students should trace through loops step by step, count iterations, predict output, and identify infinite loops (where the condition never becomes true). Common patterns: accumulating a total, counting items that meet a condition, processing each element in a list. Iteration combined with conditionals enables powerful algorithms. Vocabulary: iteration, loop, REPEAT, REPEAT UNTIL, loop body, infinite loop, iteration count, accumulator, counter, termination condition.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-2.L (Unit 3, Topic 3.9) Algorithms and Programming - 3.9 Developing Algorithms
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers developing algorithms for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.L, Unit 3, Topic 3.9). Students should understand that an algorithm is a finite set of instructions that accomplishes a specific task. Key concepts: algorithms can be expressed in natural language, pseudocode, or flowcharts; sequencing, selection (conditionals), and iteration (loops) are the three building blocks of all algorithms; existing algorithms can be combined to create new ones; there can be multiple correct algorithms for the same problem. Students should design algorithms to solve problems, express them in pseudocode, trace through them to verify correctness, and compare different algorithmic approaches. Key understanding: algorithms must be precise enough that anyone (or any computer) following the steps will get the same result. Vocabulary: algorithm, pseudocode, flowchart, sequencing, selection, iteration, step-by-step, correctness, efficiency, deterministic.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-1.C (Unit 3, Topic 3.10) Algorithms and Programming - Lists
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers lists for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-1.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.10). Students should understand that lists are ordered collections of elements that allow programs to store and process multiple related values. Key concepts: list elements are accessed by index (1-based in AP pseudocode); common operations include appending (adding to the end), inserting at a position, removing an element, and accessing/updating by index; lists can store any data type including numbers, strings, and other lists. Traversing a list with a loop processes each element; FOR EACH item IN list iterates through all elements. Students should implement algorithms that search lists, compute aggregates (sum, average, min, max), filter elements, and modify list contents. Lists are a fundamental data abstraction for the Create Performance Task. Vocabulary: list, element, index, append, insert, remove, traverse, FOR EACH, length, data abstraction, collection.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-2.P (Unit 3, Topic 3.11) Algorithms and Programming - 3.11 Binary Search
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers binary search for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-2.P, Unit 3, Topic 3.11). Students should understand that binary search is an efficient algorithm for finding an element in a sorted list by repeatedly dividing the search space in half. Key concepts: binary search requires the list to be sorted; it compares the target to the middle element and eliminates half the remaining elements; it has O(log n) efficiency, meaning it can search 1 billion items in about 30 steps; contrast with linear search which checks every element and has O(n) efficiency. Students should trace through binary search step by step, determine the maximum number of comparisons needed, and understand why sorted data enables this efficiency. Important: binary search cannot be used on unsorted data. This illustrates how algorithm choice dramatically affects performance. Vocabulary: binary search, sorted list, midpoint, search space, logarithmic, efficiency, linear search, comparison, divide and conquer.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-3.C (Unit 3, Topic 3.13) Algorithms and Programming - Developing Procedures
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers developing procedures for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-3.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.13). Students should understand how to define reusable procedures (functions) that encapsulate a sequence of instructions. Key concepts: a procedure has a name, optional parameters, and a body of instructions; procedures with a RETURN statement send a value back to the caller; procedures promote code reuse and reduce duplication; procedural abstraction hides implementation details so the caller only needs to know what the procedure does, not how. Students should write procedures that accept parameters and return values, understand that parameters act as placeholders for arguments, and recognize that breaking a program into procedures makes it more readable and maintainable. This is critical for the Create Performance Task which requires a student-developed procedure. Vocabulary: procedure, function, parameter, argument, return value, abstraction, modularity, code reuse, encapsulation.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-3.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.12) Algorithms and Programming - Calling Procedures
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers calling procedures for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-3.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.12). Students should understand how to use (call) existing procedures and interpret their behavior. Key concepts: calling a procedure executes its instructions; arguments are the values passed to parameters during the call; the return value (if any) can be stored in a variable or used in an expression; built-in procedures and library procedures provide pre-written functionality. Students should trace through procedure calls, determine what values are returned, understand that the calling code resumes after the procedure finishes, and distinguish between procedures that return values and those that perform actions (void). Properly calling procedures requires matching the number and type of arguments to the parameters. Vocabulary: procedure call, argument, parameter, return value, built-in procedure, library, execution flow, caller, invocation.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-3.E (Unit 3, Topic 3.15) Algorithms and Programming - Random Values
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers random values for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-3.E, Unit 3, Topic 3.15). Students should understand how programs generate and use random numbers to model unpredictable behavior. Key concepts: RANDOM(a, b) generates a random integer from a to b inclusive; random values introduce variability into programs; randomness is used in simulations, games, security (encryption keys), and sampling. Students should use random values to make programs behave differently each time they run, generate random selections from a range, and understand that computer-generated random numbers are actually pseudo-random (generated by deterministic algorithms). Applications: simulating dice rolls, coin flips, random sampling from a population, generating test data, and adding unpredictability to games. Vocabulary: random, pseudo-random, RANDOM function, range, simulation, variability, seed, unpredictability, uniform distribution.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-4.B (Unit 3, Topic 3.18) Algorithms and Programming - 3.18 Undecidable Problems
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers undecidable problems for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-4.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.18). Students should understand that some problems cannot be solved by any algorithm, no matter how powerful the computer. Key concepts: a decidable problem has an algorithm that always produces a correct yes/no answer in finite time; an undecidable problem has no algorithm that can solve all instances correctly; the Halting Problem (determining whether an arbitrary program will halt or run forever) is the classic example of an undecidable problem, proven by Alan Turing. Students should understand that undecidability is a fundamental limit of computing, not a matter of insufficient technology; distinguish undecidable problems from problems that are merely hard or slow to solve; and recognize that some instances of an undecidable problem may be solvable even though a general solution is impossible. Vocabulary: decidable, undecidable, Halting Problem, algorithm, solvable, Turing, computability, theoretical limits.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-4.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.17) Algorithms and Programming - Algorithmic Efficiency
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers algorithmic efficiency for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-4.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.17). Students should understand that different algorithms for the same problem can have vastly different execution times as input size grows. Key concepts: efficiency is measured by how the number of steps grows relative to input size; reasonable time algorithms (polynomial: linear, quadratic) can solve problems practically; unreasonable time algorithms (exponential, factorial) become impractical for large inputs; heuristic approaches provide approximate solutions to problems that cannot be solved efficiently. Students should compare linear search O(n) vs. binary search O(log n), understand that some problems have no known efficient solution, and recognize that a problem solvable in reasonable time on one input size may become unreasonable for larger inputs. Vocabulary: efficiency, reasonable time, unreasonable time, polynomial, exponential, heuristic, optimization, input size, scalability.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-3.F (Unit 3, Topic 3.16) Algorithms and Programming - Simulations
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers simulations for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-3.F, Unit 3, Topic 3.16). Students should understand that simulations are programs that model real-world phenomena to test hypotheses and make predictions. Key concepts: simulations use abstractions to simplify complex systems (not every detail is modeled); they often use random numbers to introduce variability; simulations enable experiments that would be too costly, dangerous, or time-consuming in the real world; the accuracy of a simulation depends on how well the model represents reality. Students should understand trade-offs in simulation design (detail vs. speed), recognize that simulations cannot perfectly predict real-world outcomes, and identify what simplifications were made. Examples: weather forecasting, traffic flow, spread of disease, physics simulations. Vocabulary: simulation, model, abstraction, hypothesis, random, variability, prediction, simplification, trade-off, real-world modeling.

Algorithms and Programming.skill: AAP-3.E (Unit 3, Topic 3.14) Algorithms and Programming - Libraries
Algorithms and Programming.prompt: This topic covers libraries for AP CSP students (CED: AAP-3.E, Unit 3, Topic 3.14). Students should understand that libraries are collections of pre-written procedures that programmers can use to extend the functionality of their programs. Key concepts: libraries save development time by providing tested, reusable code; APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) define how to interact with a library's procedures; using libraries is a form of procedural abstraction (you use the functionality without needing to understand the implementation); libraries exist for math operations, data visualization, web development, machine learning, and more. Students should understand how to import and use library procedures, read API documentation, and recognize that libraries enable programmers to build on others' work. Open-source libraries are freely available and community-maintained. Vocabulary: library, API, import, module, package, documentation, open source, reusable code, procedural abstraction, dependency.

Computer Systems and Networks.skill: CSN-1.A (Unit 4, Topic 4.1) Computer Systems and Networks - 4.1 The Internet
Computer Systems and Networks.prompt: This topic covers the Internet for AP CSP students (CED: CSN-1.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.1). Students should understand the structure and function of the Internet as a global network of interconnected networks. Key concepts: the Internet uses open protocols (TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS) that enable universal communication; data is transmitted in packets that can take different routes (packet switching); IP addresses uniquely identify devices; DNS translates domain names to IP addresses; HTTP/HTTPS governs web communication; the Internet is scalable, redundant, and decentralized by design. Students should understand the layered architecture (application, transport, network, link), how data travels from sender to receiver, and that the Internet was designed to be open and interoperable. The World Wide Web is an application built on top of the Internet, not the Internet itself. Vocabulary: Internet, protocol, TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS, IP address, packet, routing, bandwidth, latency, scalability.

Computer Systems and Networks.skill: CSN-2.A (Unit 4, Topic 4.2) Computer Systems and Networks - 4.2 Fault Tolerance
Computer Systems and Networks.prompt: This topic covers fault tolerance for AP CSP students (CED: CSN-2.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.2). Students should understand how the Internet is designed to continue functioning even when individual components fail. Key concepts: redundancy means having multiple paths between any two points in a network; if one path fails, data is rerouted through alternative paths; fault tolerance is achieved through redundant connections, not by making individual components failure-proof. Students should analyze network diagrams to determine if a network is fault tolerant (can a message still be delivered if any single connection fails?), identify single points of failure, and understand that adding redundant connections increases fault tolerance but also increases cost. The Internet's packet-switching design inherently supports fault tolerance since packets can take any available route. Vocabulary: fault tolerance, redundancy, routing, network diagram, single point of failure, packet switching, reliability, rerouting.

Computer Systems and Networks.skill: CSN-2.C (Unit 4, Topic 4.3) Computer Systems and Networks - 4.3 Parallel and Distributed Computing
Computer Systems and Networks.prompt: This topic covers parallel and distributed computing for AP CSP students (CED: CSN-2.C, Unit 4, Topic 4.3). Students should understand how tasks can be divided across multiple processors or computers to improve performance. Key concepts: sequential computing executes operations one at a time; parallel computing divides a task across multiple processors that work simultaneously; distributed computing spreads a task across multiple networked computers. Speedup from parallelism is limited by the sequential portion of the task (Amdahl's Law concept); some tasks are 'embarrassingly parallel' while others have dependencies that prevent parallelization. Students should calculate speedup for parallel solutions, identify which portions of a task can be parallelized, and understand that parallel computing does not always produce proportional speedup. Examples: web search, video rendering, scientific simulations. Vocabulary: sequential, parallel, distributed, speedup, processor, thread, dependency, synchronization, scalability, Amdahl's Law.

Impact of Computing.skill: IOC-1.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.1) Impact of Computing - 5.1 Beneficial and Harmful Effects
Impact of Computing.prompt: This topic covers beneficial and harmful effects of computing for AP CSP students (CED: IOC-1.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.1). Students should understand that computing innovations can have both positive and negative impacts on society, often simultaneously. Key concepts: innovations have intended beneficial effects but can also produce unintended harmful consequences; the same technology can affect different groups differently; effects can be social, economic, cultural, or political. Students should analyze real-world computing innovations (social media, autonomous vehicles, facial recognition, AI) and identify both benefits and harms, considering multiple stakeholder perspectives. Understanding requires looking beyond the developer's intent to actual real-world outcomes, including effects on privacy, employment, communication, and access to information. Vocabulary: computing innovation, beneficial effect, harmful effect, unintended consequence, stakeholder, societal impact, trade-off, dual-use.

Impact of Computing.skill: IOC-1.C (Unit 5, Topic 5.2) Impact of Computing - 5.2 Digital Divide
Impact of Computing.prompt: This topic covers the digital divide for AP CSP students (CED: IOC-1.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.2). Students should understand that unequal access to computing and the Internet creates and reinforces societal inequalities. Key concepts: the digital divide refers to disparities in access to technology based on geography (urban vs. rural), socioeconomic status, age, education, and country; lack of access limits educational and economic opportunities; solutions include public Internet access points, affordable devices, digital literacy programs, and infrastructure investment. Students should analyze how the digital divide affects different communities, understand that access alone is insufficient without digital literacy, and recognize that as society becomes more technology-dependent, those without access face increasing disadvantage. The digital divide exists both between and within countries. Vocabulary: digital divide, access, equity, infrastructure, broadband, digital literacy, socioeconomic, connectivity, inclusion.

Impact of Computing.skill: IOC-1.D (Unit 5, Topic 5.3) Impact of Computing - 5.3 Computing Bias
Impact of Computing.prompt: This topic covers computing bias for AP CSP students (CED: IOC-1.D, Unit 5, Topic 5.3). Students should understand that computing systems can reflect and amplify human biases. Key concepts: bias can enter computing systems through the data used to train algorithms (biased training data), through the design choices of developers (who may not represent all users), and through the assumptions embedded in algorithms; examples include facial recognition performing poorly on certain demographics, hiring algorithms discriminating against women, and search engines reinforcing stereotypes. Students should identify potential sources of bias in computing systems, understand that bias is often unintentional, and recognize strategies to mitigate bias (diverse development teams, representative data, regular auditing). Bias in computing systems can have serious real-world consequences. Vocabulary: bias, algorithm, training data, discrimination, representation, fairness, auditing, machine learning, demographic, unintentional bias.

Impact of Computing.skill: IOC-1.F (Unit 5, Topic 5.4) Impact of Computing - 5.4 Crowdsourcing
Impact of Computing.prompt: This topic covers crowdsourcing for AP CSP students (CED: IOC-1.F, Unit 5, Topic 5.4). Students should understand that crowdsourcing uses technology to harness the collective knowledge, effort, and resources of large groups of people. Key concepts: crowdsourcing leverages the Internet to gather input from many distributed contributors; examples include citizen science projects (Galaxy Zoo, Foldit), open-source software development, collaborative knowledge bases (Wikipedia), crowdfunding (Kickstarter), and distributed computing (SETI@home). Benefits include accessing diverse expertise, processing large amounts of data, and solving problems no individual could handle alone. Challenges include quality control, coordination, verifying accuracy, and ensuring representative participation. Students should analyze examples of crowdsourcing and evaluate their effectiveness and limitations. Vocabulary: crowdsourcing, open source, citizen science, distributed computing, collective intelligence, collaboration, Wikipedia, crowdfunding, volunteer computing.

Impact of Computing.skill: IOC-2.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.5) Impact of Computing - 5.5 Legal and Ethical Concerns
Impact of Computing.prompt: This topic covers legal and ethical concerns in computing for AP CSP students (CED: IOC-2.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.5). Students should understand the legal and ethical frameworks that govern the creation and use of software and digital content. Key concepts: intellectual property is protected by copyright (automatic upon creation), patents (for inventions), and trademarks (for branding); software licensing determines how programs can be used, modified, and distributed (open source vs. proprietary); Creative Commons licenses allow creators to specify permissions; digital content can be easily copied and distributed, raising piracy concerns; the ethical use of others' work requires proper attribution and respecting licenses. Students should understand fair use principles, the difference between open-source and proprietary software, and the implications of software licensing. Vocabulary: intellectual property, copyright, patent, trademark, open source, proprietary, Creative Commons, fair use, license, piracy, attribution.

Impact of Computing.skill: IOC-2.C (Unit 5, Topic 5.6) Impact of Computing - 5.6 Safe Computing
Impact of Computing.prompt: This topic covers safe computing for AP CSP students (CED: IOC-2.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.6). Students should understand how to protect personal information and maintain security in digital environments. Key concepts: personally identifiable information (PII) includes name, address, Social Security number, and data that can identify an individual; authentication methods include passwords, two-factor authentication (2FA), and biometrics; encryption transforms data so only authorized recipients can read it (symmetric vs. asymmetric keys); common threats include phishing (deceptive messages to steal credentials), malware (malicious software), and social engineering. Students should practice strong password creation, recognize phishing attempts, understand how encryption protects data in transit and at rest, and know that digital footprints (online activity records) are persistent and can affect future opportunities. Vocabulary: PII, authentication, encryption, phishing, malware, two-factor authentication, digital footprint, cybersecurity, privacy, keylogging.
